body_txt: The blacksmiths of fiction follow a certain recurring pattern. They're traditionally big, brawny men, with huge broad chests, arms bulging with muscles from days spent laboring at the forge, and necks as thick as a bull's. Their hair is almost always black as coal — sometimes gray — and their face is adorned with either a big, bristly beard or a coarse growth of stubble. A smith's work outfit consists of a long leather apron and protective gloves; beneath that, he usually goes bare-chested. He is most often seen standing outside his forge, hammering away at a piece of glowing metal. In personality, the blacksmith is a plain-spoken and rough-hewn man, but full of sensible, common wisdom. Fantasy adventurers have worse options to turn to than the town smith when they need some kind of practical advice. He's typically a Gentle Giant , disinclined to turn his immense strength against anything other than metal — unless someone makes the mistake of threatening his town or loved ones , of course. Even when he does fight, he eschews any of his forge's products and relies on his trusty, well-beaten hammer . This figure is almost always a man; a Brawn Hilda sort may turn up in this role, but this is rare. This is notable because historically, contrary to stereotypes, women were involved in the trade. While it was still male-dominated, women still owned blacksmith shops, took apprentices, worked the forge —all things that marked them as real blacksmiths. One anecdote is from William Hutton's History of Birmingham ; he encountered a nailer's shop in which he noted "one or more females, stripped of their upper garments, and not overcharged with the lower, wielding the hammer with all the grace of the sex". If a setting features Standard Fantasy Races , expect dwarves to be a good candidate for this trope, as the traditional fantasy dwarf is typically portrayed as a stout, bearded, gruff, no-nonsense sort with a talent for smithing and metalwork, and as being a lot stronger than a human despite his short height. When a blacksmith appears as a playable class in a Role-Playing Game or related media, usually as the Utility Party Member variant of the Humble Trade Class , expect the class's character art and/or portrait to mirror this trope. Compare Mighty Lumberjack .
